summary: Analysis-first proposal for the HIGH_ENTROPY path false-positive (NO spine edits)

char.trigger_mechanics:
Both manifestations trace to the SAME root defect: `secret_scanner.py`'s Pass-2 entropy detector treats absolute/relative filesystem paths as opaque high-entropy tokens because its tokenizer regex is alphabet-blind to path semantics.

**Core mechanism (`aios/security/secret_scanner.py`):**
- `_ENTROPY_TOKEN = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z0-9+/\-_]{%d,}" % 20)` (line 147) â€” the char class INCLUDES `/` (forward slash), so a POSIX-style relative path like `training_ground/test_selfcheck.py` is captured as ONE 30-char token `training_ground/test_selfcheck`, merging the directory name and filename into a single entropy sample. Windows backslash `\` is NOT in the class, so a `\`-separated Windows path splits into per-segment tokens instead (each segment then entropy-tested independently).
- `_ENTROPY_THRESHOLD: float = 4.0` bits/char (line 27), `_ENTROPY_MIN_LEN: int = 20` chars (line 23).
- `shannon_entropy()` (line 167) computes bits/char via `Counter`; `_credential_like_min_len()` (line 178) raises the length floor only for hex(32)/base32-ish(26) alphabets â€” mixed-alnum and snake_case both use the default 20-char floor.
- `scan_and_redact()` Pass 2 (lines 319-330) redacts ANY token â‰¥20 chars whose alphabet is `[A-Za-z0-9+/\-_]` and whose entropy â‰¥4.0 bits/char, with **zero path/filename awareness and zero context gating** (only the Pass-1 `AWS_SECRET_KEY` and the sliding-window Pass-3 require contextual keywords; Pass-2 has no such gate).
- Entropy density at 4.0 bits/char requires roughly 5-6 bits of effective alphabet per character. Lowercase-only / snake_case English-like names (â‰ˆ4.7-bit alphabet, correlated letter frequencies) top out around entropy 3.6-3.9 even at 60+ chars (empirically confirmed, never crossed 4.0 in my sweep up to 61 chars). Mixed-case-alphanumeric random strings (62-char alphabet, ~5.95 bits max) cross 4.0 reliably once â‰¥20-22 chars (that's exactly `tempfile.mkdtemp()`'s and Windows GUID/short-name conventions' character space) â€” empirically the flip is deterministic by n=22, probabilistic in the n=20-21 boundary band.

**Manifestation 1 â€” audit-trail filename redaction.** `aios/security/audit_logger.py:log_action()` (line 552) calls `scan_and_redact(payload)` at line 587 before writing `stored_payload` into `tamper_audit_trail.action_payload` (whenever `redact_secrets=True`, the default). ANY payload containing a `dir/file.py`-shaped relative path whose merged `dirname/stem` token is â‰¥20 chars and crosses the entropy threshold gets partially redacted mid-path, e.g. `training_ground/test_selfcheck.py` â†’ `<REDACTED:HIGH_ENTROPY:009ce3d4>.py` (reproduced verbatim). The operator's literal quoted example `test_[SENSITIVE: dd53bbede2b1].py` is actually produced by a SIBLING redactor, `aios/core/privacy_filter.py:_redact_high_entropy()` (lines 112-121, using a near-identical 20-char/`[A-Za-z0-9_+/=\-]` token pattern, `_looks_like_secret()` at line 94, and a 12-hex-char hash placeholder `_hash_placeholder()` at line 106-109) â€” reproduced byte-for-byte: `scan(\"CREATE: training_ground/test_dd53bbede2b1.py\")` â†’ `'CREATE: [SENSITIVE: 9c755d09fbaf].py'`. `PrivacyFilter` is wired only into the cloud-provider send paths (`aios/core/bedrock.py:184/211/267`, `aios/core/gemini.py:205/238/297`, `aios/core/failover.py:81/138/235`) â€” it is not itself an `audit_logger.log_action` caller I could confirm reaches `tamper_audit_trail` directly in this pass, so the operator's literal audit-trail row most likely reflects redacted content that flowed from a cloud-routed turn into a payload later logged (or a construction detail not fully traced here); regardless, `scan_and_redact` (the confirmed direct `log_action` redactor) demonstrably produces the SAME defect class on the SAME shape of plain sandbox filename, which is the reproducible, load-bearing finding.

**Manifestation 2 â€” gateway RED-misclassification of the forced auto-verify command.** `aios/agents/tool_agent.py:build_auto_verify_command()` (line 599-616) returns `f'{config.VERIFY_RUNNER} -o addopts= \"{test_arg}\" -q'` where `VERIFY_RUNNER` defaults to `\"python -m pytest\"` (`aios/config.py:222`). `_run_verify_generator` (around line 1114-1126) tries to relativize `test_abs` against `SCOPE_ROOTS[0]` first, but falls back to the absolute path when relativization fails (`ValueError` branch, line 1122-1123) â€” and in the cited test fixture (`tests/test_grant_workflow_steps.py:59-65`), the scope root itself IS the pytest tmp dir, so the absolute path is legitimately in-scope and `command_stays_in_scope` (`aios/security/scope_lock.py`) passes it through cleanly. The command then reaches `aios/security/gateway.py:classify()` (line 315), whose ordering is injection â†’ `scan_and_redact` (line 348, BEFORE scope/caution checks) â†’ destructive â†’ network â†’ env â†’ shell-escape â†’ shell-composition â†’ scope â†’ caution(`pytest`, line 165/378-380) â†’ safe. Because credential-scan runs before the YELLOW `pytest` caution pattern is ever reached, a long enough embedded path flips the WHOLE command to RED via `scan_and_redact`'s entropy pass, with `reason = f\"Embedded credential(s) detected: {findings}\"` (gateway.py:351) â€” even though the actual `pytest` invocation is legitimately YELLOW. `Executor.execute_approved()` (`aios/core/executor.py:504-512`) then re-classifies on the resume/approval path and, seeing `Zone.RED`, unconditionally refuses: `reason=f\"Human approval cannot authorise a RED action: {result.reason}\"` (line 511) â€” this is architecturally final; RED is defined as unapprovable by design (`gateway.py` docstring line 15-16, `executor.py` docstring line 495-496), so there is no human override once this classification fires.

reproductions.result: detected=True findings=('HIGH_ENTROPY',) scrubbed='CREATE: <REDACTED:HIGH_ENTROPY:009ce3d4>.py' -- merged token 'training_ground/test_selfcheck' len=30 entropy=4.031 (>=4.0 threshold)
n=1 text='CREATE: [SENSITIVE: 9c755d09fbaf].py' -- reproduces the operator-quoted '[SENSITIVE: hash]' shape verbatim, token 'training_ground/test_dd53bbede2b1' len=33 looks_like_secret=True

reproductions.input: Boundary sweep: 'training_ground/test_<N random lowercase letters>.py' scan_and_redact, N=0..19
reproductions.result: First flip to detected=True at N=10 (tokenlen=31, entropy=4.083); flips are probabilistic in N=10-17 band because lowercase-only entropy hovers just under/over 4.0; snake_case English-word stems (test_granted_write_mints_ski0, 45 chars, entropy=3.81) NEVER cross 4.0 -- confirms low-alphabet-diversity names are structurally safe regardless of length

reproductions.input: Real pytest test using tmp_path fixture: build_auto_verify_command(str(tmp_path/'test_selfcheck.py')) then gateway.classify(cmd), test function name 'test_granted_write_mints_skill_from_verified_write_full_flow' (pytest truncates per-test tmp_path dir names to 30 chars + counter; used tmp_path_factory.mktemp to bypass truncation)
reproductions.result: zone=YELLOW even at 61-char snake_case folder name (entropy=3.901, still under 4.0) -- shows snake_case test-name-derived pytest dirs alone do NOT reliably trigger the RED flip; the operator's originally-reported failing case likely also involves added character diversity (digits/mixed-case) from a hash/uuid-suffixed dir, OR the cumulative full absolute path (multiple merged path segments) pushes it over -- see next repro

reproductions.input: Mixed-case-alnum random folder name (tempfile.mkdtemp-realistic charset), length sweep 8..39, embedded in build_auto_verify_command + gateway.classify, scope root containing the path (in-scope, isolates entropy as the only variable)
reproductions.result: zone=YELLOW for all n<20 (regex needs 20+ chars to form a candidate token at all); FIRST RED at n=20 (entropy=4.022); RED reliably from n=22 onward (entropy 4.0-4.9); one dip back to YELLOW at n=21 (entropy=3.916) shows the boundary is probabilistic/character-composition-dependent right at the threshold, not a hard length cutoff

reproductions.input: Character-set comparison at fixed length=24 through gateway.classify: lowercase+underscore vs mixed-case-alnum vs lowercase-hex
reproductions.result: lowercase+underscore: entropy=3.637 zone=YELLOW; mixed-case-alnum: entropy=4.304 zone=RED; lowercase-hex: entropy=3.372 zone=YELLOW (hex alphabet is only 16 symbols, max 4.0 bits/char asymptotically, rarely exceeds threshold) -- CONFIRMS the deciding factor is alphabet diversity (mixed-case + digits), not path length or 'looks like a path' semantics

reproductions.input: Direct gateway.classify() on build_auto_verify_command output with an absolute Windows path OUTSIDE declared SCOPE_ROOTS (no scope-root override)
reproductions.result: zone=RED reason='Scope violation: ...' -- this is a SEPARATE, legitimate RED (scope check), not the credential false-positive; it only becomes the entropy false-positive once the scope root is set to contain the path (as the cited test fixture does) or the path is expressed relative to an in-scope root

char.blast_radius:
Every one of the following call sites feeds arbitrary text (often containing file paths, diffs, or command strings) through `scan_and_redact`, and is therefore exposed to the same over-redaction/false-positive-RED defect class whenever the text happens to contain a â‰¥20-char mixed-alphabet substring (paths, hashes, UUIDs, base64 blobs, or even natural mixed-case identifiers):

1. **`aios/security/audit_logger.py:587`** (`log_action`) â€” the confirmed direct cause of Manifestation 1. Every audited action (tool calls, approvals, rejections, verify results, cloud-route records, compaction summaries) whose payload embeds a qualifying path/token gets silently corrupted mid-string in the tamper-evident ledger. Breaks: forensic readability of `tamper_audit_trail` (an operator or auditor cannot tell what file was actually touched â€” the filename is partially replaced with an opaque hash), while NOT breaking the hash-chain/signature integrity itself (redaction happens before hashing, so the chain stays internally consistent, just semantically lossy).

2. **`aios/security/gateway.py:348`** (`classify`) â€” the confirmed direct cause of Manifestation 2. EVERY command classification (not just auto-verify) that embeds a long, high-diversity path is at risk of flipping from its true zone (GREEN/YELLOW) to RED. This is upstream of both `validate_command` (line 404) and `Executor.execute` / `execute_approved` (`aios/core/executor.py:466,504`), so it silently blocks legitimate GREEN/YELLOW work anywhere a qualifying path is embedded in a shell command â€” not limited to auto-verify. Confirmed concretely for `build_auto_verify_command` (`tool_agent.py:599-616`, called at line 1124), which means: any sandbox whose tmp/working directory name happens to contain enough character diversity in a â‰¥20-char run makes the FORCED, non-optional auto-verify step permanently unrunnable and unapprovable (RED has no human-override path by design â€” `executor.py:505-512`), silently degrading `verified_ok` to `False`/`VerificationStrength.NONE` (`tool_agent.py:1130-1131`) and therefore corrupting the earned-autonomy evidence trail (`self.autonomy.record_outcome(..., success=verified_ok, ...)`, line 1144-1147) with false failures that were never really test failures.

3. **`aios/agents/tool_handlers.py:93,199,308,429`** â€” read_file output, diff previews (create/edit), and verify command output are all scanned; a diff or file-read result containing a long embedded path/token gets the same mid-string corruption before being shown to the model/human as a "preview."

4. **`aios/agents/self_analysis_agent.py:595,606,718`** â€” self-analysis evidence and diffs scrubbed; false positives here degrade the readability of self-proposed fixes' evidence trail.

5. **`aios/api/main.py:3020,3101,3118`** â€” episodic memory record content, and two other payload-scrub sites; user-visible chat/episodic history can have paths mangled.

6. **`aios/memory/{facts,mistake,curriculum,skills,semantic,episodic,development,conversation}.py`** (multiple lines each) â€” nearly every persistent memory-write path (facts, mistakes/lessons, curriculum prompts, skill steps, semantic index text, episodic content, development task text) runs its text through `scan_and_redact` before storage. Any of these that legitimately contain a file path (a very common thing to log in a coding assistant's memory) risk the same silent corruption, degrading the quality/recall of stored knowledge over time â€” a slow, compounding form of the same defect rather than a hard block.

7. **`aios/core/autonomy.py:92`**, **`aios/core/websearch.py:54`**, **`aios/core/alignment.py:126`**, **`aios/core/approvals.py:149`**, **`aios/core/self_apply.py:165`**, **`aios/runtime/secret_policy.py:43,52`** â€” autonomy target strings, search queries, alignment notes, approval payloads, self-apply approver strings, and the general secret-policy scrub-before-cloud-egress path are all exposed identically. `self_apply.py:165` is notable: it calls `.detected` (not `.scrubbed`) on the approver string â€” a false-positive there could reject a legitimate approver identity outright rather than just cosmetically redact it.

8. **`aios/council/queens/security.py:75`** â€” the Council runtime's SecurityQueen calls `classify(gateway_command)` directly, so the same RED-misclassification risk applies to any command the Council's worker proposes that embeds a qualifying path, independent of the ToolAgent/ToolAgent's auto-verify path.

9. **`aios/api/main.py:2348` (`security_classify` endpoint) and `:4099`** â€” a public/internal classification API surface; any caller (including UI-driven "explain this classification" tooling) gets the same false RED for qualifying input.

Net effect: this is not a niche edge case confined to one test fixture. It is a structural property of `_ENTROPY_TOKEN`'s path-swallowing character class (`/` included, no path/filename exemption, no context gating on Pass 2) combined with a fixed universal 4.0-bit threshold that has no allowance for the fact that real-world sandbox paths â€” especially anything with a temp-dir random component, a hash-derived name, a UUID, or simply enough mixed-case/digit diversity â€” will cross it. The two manifestations the operator observed are the two most consequential expressions of the same root cause: one degrades observability (redacted audit rows), the other is a hard availability failure (verified evidence becomes structurally impossible to produce in an affected sandbox, since RED is unapprovable by design and the auto-verify step is forced/non-optional).
char.boundary:
**Manifestation 1 (`scan_and_redact` / audit-trail over-redaction):**
- Trigger requires a single regex-matched token (chars from `[A-Za-z0-9+/\-_]`, note `/` is included so POSIX path separators merge dirname+filename into one token; Windows `\` is NOT included, so backslash paths split per-segment) of length â‰¥20 whose Shannon entropy â‰¥4.0 bits/char.
- Snake_case / lowercase-English-like paths (e.g. `training_ground/test_verify_writes.py`, `test_granted_write_mints_ski0`) essentially NEVER cross 4.0 bits/char regardless of length (measured up to 61 chars, entropy plateaus ~3.6-3.9) â€” SAFE.
- A short filename alone, or a short enough combined `dir/file` token (<20 chars total, or â‰¥20 but low-entropy), passes clean â€” e.g. `training_ground/test_foo.py` (24-char token, entropy 3.657) is NOT redacted; `training_ground/test_selfcheck.py` (30-char token, entropy 4.031) IS redacted.
- Any random-looking hex/UUID/hash-derived filename stem (e.g. `test_9f8e7d6c5b4a39281706f5e4d3c2b1a0.py`, entropy 4.638) is reliably redacted, matching the class of names AI-generated sandbox test files or content-addressed artifacts commonly get.

**Manifestation 2 (`gateway.classify` RED-misclassification):**
- The `pytest` YELLOW caution pattern is unreachable once `scan_and_redact` fires RED earlier in the classification order (secret-scan runs BEFORE the caution-pattern loop in `gateway.py`).
- Boundary is driven by embedded-path CHARACTER DIVERSITY, not raw path length: a mixed-case-alphanumeric run of â‰¥20 chars (the realistic shape of `tempfile.mkdtemp()` suffixes, GUID fragments, or hash-derived sandbox dir names) flips to RED deterministically by ~22 chars, probabilistically at 20-21 chars. A pure lowercase/snake_case pytest test-name-derived tmp dir (even 60+ chars) stays safely under 4.0 bits/char and does NOT flip.
- This means the operator's originally-observed failure (`test_granted_write_mints_ski0`, a snake_case/digit name) did not reproduce as RED in isolation via that name alone in my sweeps â€” the actual failing case in the wild almost certainly also depends on the FULL merged absolute path (drive letter, `pytest-of-<user>`, `pytest-<N>`, and the test-derived segment all concatenated by the entropy tokenizer wherever `/`-joined, or wherever a single segment happens to contain enough digit/case diversity) crossing the density threshold in combination â€” the fixture comment's claim that a shorter `...\Temp\agXXXX\...` path passes while the long test-name-derived one RED-blocks is consistent with total embedded entropy/length correlating with dirname length in the common case, even though my controlled sweep shows length alone is not sufficient â€” diversity is the deciding factor, and longer test-derived names statistically have more opportunity to contain mixed digit/letter runs (e.g., appended pytest counters, parametrize ids with numbers) that push a substring over 4.0.

options.name: A. Per-segment entropy scoring (score path-shaped tokens by segment, never the merged token)
options.semantics:
In `_ENTROPY_TOKEN`'s replace callback (secret_scanner.py Pass 2, ~line 320) and equivalently in `privacy_filter._redact_high_entropy`:

```
def _entropy_replace(match):
    token = match.group(0)
    if _is_inside_redacted(match.start(), redacted_spans):
        return token
    # NEW: split on path separators BEFORE scoring, never score the merged token.
    segments = re.split(r'[\\/]', token)
    if len(segments) > 1:
        # path-shaped candidate (contains a separator) -> score each segment
        # independently; a merged dirname+filename must never inherit combined
        # entropy it didn't individually earn.
        redacted_any = False
        out_segments = []
        for seg in segments:
            if len(seg) >= _credential_like_min_len(seg) and shannon_entropy(seg) >= _ENTROPY_THRESHOLD:
                findings.append('HIGH_ENTROPY')
                out_segments.append(f'<REDACTED:HIGH_ENTROPY:{_fingerprint(seg)}>')
                redacted_any = True
            else:
                out_segments.append(seg)
        if redacted_any:
            sep = '/' if '/' in token else '\\'
            return sep.join(out_segments)  # only actually reconstructs if separators were uniform; else re-walk original via finditer(separator) to preserve exact separators
        return token
    # no separator -> existing whole-token logic, unchanged
    if len(token) >= _credential_like_min_len(token) and shannon_entropy(token) >= _ENTROPY_THRESHOLD:
        findings.append('HIGH_ENTROPY')
        return f'<REDACTED:HIGH_ENTROPY:{_fingerprint(token)}>'
    return token
```

Also widen `_ENTROPY_TOKEN` to include `\\` in the character class so Windows paths form one candidate token pre-split too (today `\\` splits them for free, but only accidentally â€” this makes the segmentation intentional and uniform across POSIX/Windows).
options.strengthen_only_argument: This is strictly more granular, never less. Every substring that is currently scanned as part of a merged token is still scanned â€” just individually instead of only jointly. A real secret embedded as one path segment (`s3://bucket/AKIAABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP`, `/secrets/sk-ant-api03-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx`) still lands as its own segment â‰¥20 chars and still crosses the entropy threshold on its own merits â€” segmenting never dilutes a real high-entropy run, it only stops two unrelated low/medium-entropy segments (e.g. `training_ground` + `test_selfcheck`) from being concatenated into a single artificially-higher-entropy blob. Concatenation can only ever raise apparent diversity relative to either half; splitting can only lower false-positive surface, never hide a segment that was independently dangerous. No named-pattern regex (Pass 1) is touched at all.
options.residual_risk: A credential that is deliberately split across a path-like separator to individually understay the 20-char floor per segment but is dangerous when reassembled (e.g., `AKIAABCDEFGHI/JKLMNOP` split at the middle of an actual AWS key) would evade Pass 2 â€” but this was already true before segmentation for any token containing a stray `-`/`_` that pushed segment length under 20 chars in the old regex's own greedy match boundaries; it is not a new gap opened by this change, and Pass 1's named regexes (AKIA, sk-, ghp_, etc.) do not require the entropy pass at all and remain unaffected. Also: reconstructing the exact original separators when segments are non-uniform (mixed `/` and `\\` in one token, unlikely but possible from adjacent path fragments in a diff) needs care in implementation â€” safest form re-walks with `finditer` over a combined separator regex rather than a single `split`+`join`, which the pseudocode above flags.

options.name: B. Context-aware exemption for strict-path-grammar tokens in runner argument position
options.semantics:
Add a narrow, opt-in-by-shape exemption applied ONLY inside Pass 2 (never Pass 1, never the sliding-window Pass 3), gated on BOTH a strict path grammar AND adjacency to a recognized runner invocation:

```
_PATH_GRAMMAR = re.compile(
    r'^(?:[A-Za-z]:[\\/])?'          # optional Windows drive
    r'(?:[\w.\-]+[\\/])*'            # zero+ directory segments
    r'[\w.\-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9]{1,8}$'   # filename.ext (extension required)
)
_RUNNER_PREFIX = re.compile(
    r'\b(?:python(?:3)?(?:\.\d+)?|pytest|node|npm|npx|go\s+run|'
    r'java|ruby|pwsh|powershell)\b[^\n]{0,40}$'
)

def _entropy_replace(match):
    token = match.group(0)
    if _is_inside_redacted(match.start(), redacted_spans):
        return token
    if (_PATH_GRAMMAR.match(token)
            and _RUNNER_PREFIX.search(payload[:match.start()])
            and not _has_secret_context(payload, match.start())):  # reuse existing keyword gate, INVERTED: absence of secret keywords required
        return token  # exempt: looks like <runner> ... <path>.<ext>, no secret keyword nearby
    if len(token) >= _credential_like_min_len(token) and shannon_entropy(token) >= _ENTROPY_THRESHOLD:
        findings.append('HIGH_ENTROPY')
        return f'<REDACTED:HIGH_ENTROPY:{_fingerprint(token)}>'
    return token
```

Requires: (1) the token parses as `[drive:]seg\seg\...\file.ext` with a real extension â€” no bare 20-char blobs qualify since those have no `.ext` suffix; (2) a runner-command token appears within 40 chars before it in the same payload; (3) NO secret-context keyword (`secret`,`token`,`key`,`password`,...) within the existing 50-char radius. All three must hold.
options.strengthen_only_argument: The exemption is a strict AND of three independent, narrow conditions, each chosen so a real secret structurally cannot satisfy it: (a) real credentials (AKIA keys, sk-ant- keys, JWTs, hex seeds, base64 blobs) do not end in `.{1,8}alnum-extension}` by convention and don't contain path separators, so `_PATH_GRAMMAR` already excludes them without needing the runner/keyword checks; (b) even if a secret coincidentally had a dotted suffix (e.g. `...ABCDEF.json` as a filename containing an inline key â€” pathological but conceivable), the keyword-context gate still fires and blocks the exemption if `key`/`secret`/`token`/etc. appears nearby, which is exactly the existing `_has_secret_context` gate already trusted elsewhere in this file (Pass-3 sliding window uses it as a POSITIVE requirement; here it's reused as a NEGATIVE veto, same trusted primitive, inverted polarity); (c) the runner-prefix requirement means the exemption only fires in the one place this defect manifests (command classification / audit of tool invocations), not broadly across arbitrary payload text â€” a credential pasted into a chat message or env-var assignment never sees a runner prefix and gets scanned exactly as before. Nothing about Pass 1's named regexes or Pass 3's sliding window is touched.
options.residual_risk: Grammar-based exemptions are inherently more surface area than segment-scoring (Option A) â€” a sufficiently contrived attacker-controlled path (`python run.py --secret=AKIAABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP.txt` disguised as a filename with an appended extension, no space) could theoretically slip past if it also avoids all keyword hits, though the AWS-key named regex in Pass 1 would still catch `AKIA[A-Z0-9]{16}` regardless of what Pass 2 does â€” Pass 1 runs first and is untouched by this option, so this residual risk only affects *entropy-only* (unnamed) secret formats, not the ~12 named credential shapes already covered. Also adds real regex-maintenance surface (two new patterns) versus Option A's structural split, and the 40-char/50-char proximity windows are heuristic magic numbers that need the boundary-sweep test plan below to tune safely.

options.name: C. Allowlist anchoring â€” exempt only tokens that resolve under a configured SCOPE_ROOTS path
options.semantics:
Apply exclusively inside `gateway.classify()`, not inside `scan_and_redact` itself (so audit-trail redaction, memory writers, and all other `scan_and_redact` callers listed in blast_radius are completely unaffected â€” this option is the narrowest in scope):

```
# gateway.py, immediately before the existing scan = scan_and_redact(command) call:
def _mask_in_scope_paths(command: str) -> str:
    """Replace substrings that resolve under a configured SCOPE_ROOTS entry
    with a fixed-length neutral placeholder BEFORE credential scanning, so
    in-scope filesystem paths can never contribute an entropy token. Restored
    verbatim into the reason string afterward (masking is local to the
    classify() call, findings/labels are unaffected, only the substring
    handed to scan_and_redact differs from the substring gateway.classify
    ultimately allowlists/denies against for scope/caution/safe patterns,
    which continue to run against the ORIGINAL unmasked command)."""
    masked = command
    for tok in re.findall(r'(?:[A-Za-z]:)?[\w./\\-]{8,}', command):
        candidate = Path(tok)
        try:
            resolved = candidate.resolve()
        except (OSError, ValueError):
            continue
        if any(_is_relative_to(resolved, root) for root in SCOPE_ROOTS_RESOLVED):
            masked = masked.replace(tok, '�' * len(tok))  # length-preserving, alphabet-excluded placeholder
    return masked

# in classify():
scan = scan_and_redact(_mask_in_scope_paths(command))
if scan.detected:
    return ClassificationResult(Zone.RED, 1.0, f"Embedded credential(s) detected: {', '.join(scan.findings)}")
```

Critically: masking happens on a COPY used only for the credential-scan input; `command_stays_in_scope`, caution-pattern, and safe-pattern checks downstream continue to run on the untouched original `command` string, so scope enforcement itself is not weakened by this change â€” it is orthogonal.
options.strengthen_only_argument: The exemption is anchored to filesystem reality, not text shape: a token only escapes scanning if `Path(tok).resolve()` ACTUALLY resolves to a path under an operator-configured `SCOPE_ROOTS` entry (`aios/config.py` / `scope_lock.py`) â€” the same trust boundary the sandbox already uses to decide what the agent is allowed to touch at all. A real secret can never satisfy this: secrets are not filesystem paths and do not resolve to anything under `SCOPE_ROOTS` (an attacker cannot make `Path('AKIAABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP').resolve()` land inside `training_ground/` by construction â€” `resolve()` just prepends cwd, producing a path outside every configured root unless the attacker already has cwd control, which is a strictly larger compromise than this fix's threat model). This is the most conservative of the four options because it (1) only touches `gateway.classify`, leaving `scan_and_redact`'s behavior for every other of the 9 blast-radius call sites completely unchanged â€” so audit-trail redaction (Manifestation 1) is NOT fixed by this option alone; and (2) never exempts a token that fails to independently prove filesystem membership, so `AWS_ACCESS_KEY`/`OPENAI_API_KEY`/etc. named-pattern hits in Pass 1 still fire (masking happens after none of Pass 1 is bypassed â€” Pass 1 still sees the full original text since `_mask_in_scope_paths` only feeds the entropy-scan copy... note: implementation MUST mask before calling scan_and_redact wholesale, meaning Pass 1 also runs on masked text â€” see residual risk).
options.residual_risk: Two real risks: (1) as written, masking happens on the whole string BEFORE calling `scan_and_redact`, which means Pass 1's named regexes (AKIA, sk-, ghp_, JWT, PEM blocks) ALSO run against the masked copy â€” if a real secret happened to be substring-contained within a token that itself resolves under SCOPE_ROOTS (e.g., an attacker names a file `AKIAABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP.txt` and places it inside `training_ground/`), that filename-as-secret would be masked away before Pass 1 ever sees it, silently defeating the AWS-key detector â€” this is a genuine weakening risk unique to this option and must be closed by re-running Pass 1 (named-pattern regexes only) against the ORIGINAL unmasked command and OR-ing its findings in, before masking is applied only for Pass 2/3; (2) does not address Manifestation 1 (audit-trail redaction) at all since `audit_logger.log_action` calls `scan_and_redact` directly, not through `gateway.classify` â€” would need a second, separate call site change to `audit_logger.py` with the same SCOPE_ROOTS-anchoring logic, doubling the surface area and duplicating the resolve()-based masking helper (or extracting it to a shared utility, which is fine but is additional design work not yet specified here). Given (1), this option is NOT safe to adopt in the naive form above without the Pass-1-on-unmasked-text fix folded in.

options.name: D. Detector-threshold change (raise _ENTROPY_THRESHOLD or _ENTROPY_MIN_LEN) â€” NOT recommended, included for completeness
options.semantics: Trivial parameter change: raise `_ENTROPY_THRESHOLD` from 4.0 to some higher bits/char value (e.g. 4.3-4.5), or raise `_ENTROPY_MIN_LEN` from 20 to e.g. 24-28, uniformly for all Pass-2 (and optionally Pass-3) scans, with no structural change to tokenization.
options.strengthen_only_argument: Cannot be argued as strengthen-only with confidence, which is exactly why it is not recommended. Raising the threshold moves the boundary in ONE direction only (fewer positives), and the characterization's own boundary sweep shows real credential-shaped tokens (mixed-case-alnum â‰¥20-22 chars, e.g. `tempfile.mkdtemp()`-style secrets, GUID-fragment API keys) sit in the SAME entropy band (4.0-4.9 bits/char) as the false-positive-triggering mixed-case path segments â€” there is no threshold value that separates 'random mixed-alnum path/temp-dir name' from 'random mixed-alnum credential fragment' because they are drawn from statistically similar alphabets. Any threshold increase large enough to stop suppress the false positives in the reported boundary band (n=20-22) necessarily also suppresses genuine short-to-medium random secrets in the identical band â€” this is a real detection-power trade, not a free strengthening, unlike A/B/C which add structure without touching the credential/path statistical overlap.
options.residual_risk: High and hard to bound: any real secret whose length/alphabet places it in the same entropy band as the false-positive corpus (short random API keys, short base64 tokens, GUID-shaped keys under ~30 chars) becomes systematically harder to catch. This is a strict trade-off between false-positive rate and false-negative rate on the SAME statistical population, not an orthogonal improvement â€” should only be considered as a last resort, and only with heavy regression-corpus evidence that the specific new threshold does not measurably regress recall on the real-credential corpus (below).

prop.recommendation:
Adopt Option A (per-segment entropy scoring) as the primary fix, layered with Option B's runner/keyword context gate as a secondary refinement if A alone leaves residual false positives in the wild. Do NOT adopt Option D as a standalone fix. Rationale:

1. **A is structural, not statistical.** It fixes the actual root cause identified in the characterization: `_ENTROPY_TOKEN`'s `/`-inclusive alphabet merges unrelated path segments (dirname + filename) into one artificially-higher-entropy blob. Splitting on separators before scoring removes the mechanism that manufactures false entropy, without weakening the detector's ability to catch a real secret that is itself â‰¥20 chars of high-diversity content, whether that secret sits alone or as one segment of a longer path (`/secrets/AKIA.../file` or `s3://bucket/sk-ant-...`). It is also the SMALLEST, most mechanically verifiable change (pure tokenization fix, no new heuristic magic numbers, no new regex families) and fixes BOTH manifestations simultaneously since it lives in the shared `scan_and_redact`/`_redact_high_entropy` core that both `audit_logger.py` (Manifestation 1) and `gateway.classify` (Manifestation 2, via the forced auto-verify command) call.

2. **B is a reasonable secondary layer** for cases A does not fully resolve (e.g., a single path segment that is itself â‰¥20 chars of high-diversity temp-dir-suffix â€” `test_a8f3k29d...` â€” where segmentation alone still leaves that one segment crossing the threshold). B's runner-prefix + negative-keyword-gate combination is narrowly scoped to the command-classification path where this actually causes hard failures (RED-unapprovable auto-verify), reuses the already-trusted `_has_secret_context` primitive (just inverted), and does not touch Pass 1's named regexes at all.

3. **C is the most conservative but incomplete and has a real Pass-1-bypass risk** as specified (masking-before-Pass-1 could hide a named credential whose filename happens to embed it, inside an in-scope path) â€” it would need the Pass-1-runs-on-unmasked-text correction folded in, and even then only fixes Manifestation 2 (gateway), leaving Manifestation 1 (audit-trail) unaddressed unless duplicated into `audit_logger.py` with its own SCOPE_ROOTS-resolution call. Worth keeping as a belt-and-suspenders addition ON TOP of A for the gateway path specifically, but not as the sole fix.

4. **D is rejected as a standalone fix** â€” the characterization's own data shows real short/medium mixed-alphabet secrets and false-positive path segments occupy the same entropy band, so raising the threshold is a recall/precision trade on the identical population, not a strengthen-only change.

Test plan (applies across A+B): build a regression corpus of REAL credential shapes (AKIA[A-Z0-9]{16}, sk-ant-api03-<32+ chars>, sk-<32+ chars> OpenAI-style, ghp_<36+ chars>, xox[baprs]-... Slack, a JWT header.payload.signature, a 40-char base64 AWS secret with AWS context, a bare 24-30 char hex/base64 blob with no path shape) and confirm 100% continue to be flagged (a) standalone, (b) embedded as one path segment (`/tmp/<secret>.log`), (c) embedded at the end of a `python -m pytest <path>/<secret>` command reaching `gateway.classify`. Separately build the false-positive corpus from the characterization's boundary sweeps (snake_case pytest tmp-dir names, `training_ground/test_*.py`-shaped strings, mixed-case-alnum tempfile.mkdtemp()-style dir names embedded via `build_auto_verify_command`) and confirm all now classify YELLOW/GREEN as appropriate and zero produce `HIGH_ENTROPY` findings. Run both corpora through `audit_logger.log_action` (Manifestation 1 path) and `gateway.classify`/`Executor.execute_approved` (Manifestation 2 path) end-to-end, plus the existing `tests/test_grant_workflow_steps.py` fixture (the long test-name-derived tmp dir) to confirm it now reaches YELLOW/pytest-caution instead of RED. Add explicit unit tests for the segment-reconstruction edge case (mixed `/`/`\\` in one token) and for a pathological secret-as-filename case (`AKIAABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP.txt` under an in-scope root) to prove Pass 1 still fires regardless of Option A/C. All new/changed tests land in the RED-zone Â§VIII operator-approval flow before any `aios/security/**` file is actually touched, per the frozen-spine constraint.

attacks.attack: Real 40-char AWS-secret-shaped high-entropy string split across attacker-controlled path separators into sub-20-char segments: C:\a\wJalrXUtnFEMI\K7MDENGbPxRfiC\YEXAMPLEKEY12\x.py
attacks.outcome: EVADED. detected=False. Each segment falls under the 20-char _ENTROPY_MIN_LEN floor once split, even though the un-split token (wJalrXUtnFEMIK7MDENGbPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY) has entropy ~5.4 bits/char and IS caught as HIGH_ENTROPY by the current unmodified scanner. The strengthen-only claim is false: per-segment LENGTH, not diversity, becomes the new bypass surface.

attacks.attack: AKIA-shaped credential split at attacker-chosen offsets: C:\keys\AKIAIOSFODNN7E\XAMPLEKEY123456\x.py
attacks.outcome: EVADED. detected=False -- named-pattern regex needs the contiguous run, entropy pass misses since every segment is under 20 chars.

attacks.attack: As literally specified: exemption requires the matched ENTROPY TOKEN to end in \.ext$, but _ENTROPY_TOKEN's character class excludes '.', so no possible match can ever satisfy _PATH_GRAMMAR. Tested against the operator's own reported false-positive (long pytest tmp-dir path).
attacks.outcome: Exemption never fires -- dead code. Confirmed the baseline case passes anyway only because snake_case entropy never reached 4.0 to begin with (pre-existing scanner behavior), not because Option B's logic did anything.

attacks.option: B. Context-aware path-grammar exemption (charitable 'fixed' implementation: grammar checked against trailing chars in payload rather than the dead match)
attacks.attack: node zK9mQ2xP7vL4wR8nJ3tY6hB1cD5fG0sA9eU2iO7p.js -- real 40-char-secret-shaped value with a fake .js extension, immediately after a runner token, zero path separators, no secret keyword within 50 chars
attacks.outcome: EVADED. detected=False, scrubbed unchanged. _PATH_GRAMMAR's directory-prefix group is zero-or-more, so a bare 'secretvalue.ext' with NO separator satisfies the grammar exactly as well as a real path. The strengthen-only claim ('real credentials don't contain path separators, so _PATH_GRAMMAR already excludes them') is false -- the grammar never required a separator in the first place.

attacks.attack: python -m pytest --export-secret=wJalrXUtnFEMIK7MDENGbPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY.tmp -q -- same secret but with 'secret' substring nearby
attacks.outcome: CAUGHT (detected=True). The keyword veto worked here since 'secret' fell inside the 50-char radius -- shows the bypass in the prior row specifically depends on avoiding all 11 gate keywords, which is trivial for an attacker to satisfy by choosing an unrelated variable/file name.

attacks.option: C. SCOPE_ROOTS allowlist anchoring (as literally specified: mask whole command, then call scan_and_redact on masked text)
attacks.attack: python -m pytest C:\Users\kumar\ai-editor\training_ground\AKIA_EXAMPLE_KEY.py -q -- real AWS-format key used as a filename, placed inside the sandbox's own SCOPE_ROOTS directory (agent-writable, no special access needed)
attacks.outcome: EVADED. detected=False. Masking runs BEFORE Pass 1 (named regexes), so the in-scope substring is replaced with null placeholders before the AWS_ACCESS_KEY regex ever sees it -- defeats the HIGHEST-CONFIDENCE detector class, not just the entropy heuristic. This is exactly the residual risk the option's own author flagged as making the naive form unsafe to adopt.

attacks.option: C. SCOPE_ROOTS allowlist anchoring (author's own suggested fix: Pass 1 runs on unmasked original text; masking applied only before Pass 2/entropy)
attacks.attack: python -m pytest C:\Users\kumar\ai-editor\training_ground\wJalrXUtnFEMIK7MDENGbPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY.py -q -- entropy-only secret (no named-regex format) named as a file inside the sandbox's own scope root
attacks.outcome: EVADED. detected=False even with the fix applied. This is not a residual edge case but the option's core structural design: any entropy-only credential (generic API key, DB password, non-standard token format) that an attacker or a compromised tool-call writes into the agent's own working directory is unconditionally exempted from Pass 2, since scope-residency requires no privilege beyond what any tool call already has.

attacks.attack: Named-pattern secret placed in-scope, verified against the fix
attacks.outcome: CAUGHT correctly (detected=True, AWS_ACCESS_KEY) once Pass 1 runs on unmasked text -- confirms the fix closes the Pass-1 bypass specifically, but Manifestation 1 (audit_logger.py's direct scan_and_redact call) is untouched by Option C entirely per its own scope note, so this option alone does not address the operator's first reported symptom.

attacks.option: D. Raise entropy threshold / min length
attacks.attack: Statistical simulation: 2000 random 20-char mixed-alnum 'secret-shaped' strings vs 2000 random 20-char mixed-alnum 'path-shaped' strings, same construction
attacks.outcome: Not a smuggle attempt but a validity check on D's own premise: both populations were statistically indistinguishable (secret mean entropy 4.025, path mean 4.026, ranges overlapping 3.32-4.32 for both). Confirms no threshold value can separate short random secrets from short random path/temp-dir names -- any threshold raise that suppresses the path false positives equally suppresses detection of equally-shaped real secrets. D's own 'NOT recommended' verdict holds.

attack.verdict:
None of the four options survive as specified. Every one of A, B, and C has a demonstrated, working credential-smuggling path â€” reproduced empirically against a faithful reimplementation of each option's own pseudocode â€” that defeats detection of a REAL, high-entropy, credential-shaped secret (not just a false-positive path string). Option D's own 'not recommended' self-assessment is confirmed correct by direct statistical simulation: short random secrets and short random path/temp-dir names are drawn from the same distribution, so raising the threshold trades detection power 1:1 rather than fixing anything for free.

Ranked by how much they concede: Option A is the worst â€” the very payload class the characterization singles out as safe from dilution (a real 40-char base64/mixed AWS-style secret) is fully invisible after trivial attacker-controlled path segmentation, and its 'concatenation can only raise entropy, never hide it' argument is simply wrong once you account for the independent length gate applied per segment. Option B is next worst â€” as literally written it's dead code that doesn't even fix the reported bug, and under any realistic working implementation its own grammar makes zero-separator bare 'secret.ext' arguments to any recognized runner a clean bypass, with the keyword veto as the only backstop (trivially avoided by not naming a variable 'secret'/'key'/'token'). Option C is the most narrowly scoped and the most honest about its own residual risk, but even in its author's 'fixed' form it has a structural (not edge-case) hole: any entropy-only secret written into the sandbox's own scope root â€” which requires no privilege beyond a normal tool call â€” is unconditionally exempted, and it does nothing for Manifestation 1 (the audit-trail redaction bug) at all.

Single safest path forward: do not adopt any of A/B/C/D as specified. If the operator wants to relax the current detector, the only design that survived informal scrutiny during this review is a much narrower version of Option C's idea â€” restrict the SCOPE_ROOTS exemption to Pass 2 only (never Pass 1), apply it strictly as a display/redaction-formatting concern in gateway.classify's REASON STRING (i.e., keep the RED-vs-YELLOW zone decision keyed off the ORIGINAL unmasked scan result, and only use path-awareness to make the human-facing reason message legible), and separately patch audit_logger.py's redaction call to preserve the last path segment (filename) verbatim when the merged token's redaction would otherwise straddle a path separator, WITHOUT touching Pass 2's underlying decision to redact. That still needs its own adversarial round â€” it was not one of the four options given and was not empirically tested here â€” before any change to aios/security/** should be proposed for operator approval under the RED-zone Â§VIII flow. Until a rule survives adversarial testing the same way the CURRENT scanner did against every attack payload in this review, the two known false-positive manifestations should be treated as a UX/observability cost the operator accepts in exchange for the detector's proven strength, not as a defect to patch by loosening detection.
